message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding C-6: Bootstrap connection context-manager may COMMIT/return a connection left in a broken state if advisory-unlock and DDL both fail, with the pool reusing it
File: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:283
Description: In get_checkpointer, `with pool.connection() as conn:` acquires the advisory lock, runs setup() DDL, then best-effort unlocks in finally (swallowing errors). If setup() raises, the outer `except Exception` closes and de-registers the whole pool (good). But the unlock is swallowed unconditionally, and on autocommit the connection is then returned to the pool by the context manager even though it may still hold the session advisory lock (if pg_advisory_unlock failed e.g. due to a transient error) or carry the leaked lock_timeout GUC (see C-1). A returned connection that still holds the migration advisory lock will, on its next checkout for a real checkpoint op, keep blocking any OTHER process trying to bootstrap the same schema — the very cross-process serialization the lock provides is undermined because the lock is held by a connection now doing unrelated work. The auto-release-on-connection-return comment assumes the connection is CLOSED on return, but a pooled connection is reset/reused, not closed, so the session (and its advisory lock) persists.
PoC: Force pg_advisory_unlock to raise (monkeypatch conn.execute to throw only on the unlock statement). setup() succeeds, pool is cached and returned. The bootstrap connection retains the session advisory lock and is checked back into the pool. A second process calling get_checkpointer() on the same DB blocks on pg_advisory_lock for the full lock_timeout because process 1's pooled (idle) connection still holds it.

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

240	    # Fast path: reuse a live, self-healing pool for this conn_str rather than opening a new pool
241	    # (and its background worker thread) on every call (#144). The liveness check on checkout
242	    # replaces dead connections, so a cached pool stays valid across transient outages.

247	    # Serialize bootstrap PER conn_str — NOT process-globally (#129). Unrelated databases
248	    # initialize concurrently, and a stuck advisory lock on one conn_str can never block init of
249	    # another. The brief meta-lock (_init_lock) is only taken inside the helpers, never held
250	    # across the blocking advisory-lock wait / DDL below.

263	            min_size = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1")))
264	            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8")))

274	                    "connect_timeout": connect_timeout,

276	                check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)

281	            # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed

283	            with pool.connection() as conn:

202	def _acquire_migration_lock(conn: Any) -> None:
203	    """Acquire the cross-process schema-migration advisory lock with a bounded wait (#129).
204	
205	    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
206	    so a stuck/crashed initializer cannot hang bootstrap indefinitely; the resulting error
207	    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
208	    (not ``SET``) so the value can be safely parameterized.
209	    """
210	    timeout_ms = _migration_lock_timeout_ms()
211	    if timeout_ms:
212	        conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", (str(timeout_ms),))
213	    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))

content.text:
psycopg_pool >=3.2. This is the crux. In psycopg3's `ConnectionPool`, when a connection is returned to the pool via the context manager (`putconn`), the pool calls `reset()` on the connection. Let me recall psycopg3's documented reset behavior.

In psycopg3's `ConnectionPool._return_connection` → `_reset_connection`, the default reset does:
- `conn.rollback()` if in a transaction
- If `autocommit`/isolation/readonly/deferrable differ from pool defaults, it resets them
- Critically, psycopg3's pool reset runs `ROLLBACK` but does NOT issue `DISCARD ALL` or `RESET ALL` by default.

So the advisory lock (session-scoped) and the `lock_timeout` GUC would NOT be cleared by the default pool reset. That actually supports the finding. But let me verify the actual default reset behavior precisely, and check whether the project configures a custom `reset` handler.

content.text:
No custom `reset`/`configure` handler is set on the pool. So the default psycopg3 pool reset applies, which does NOT clear session advisory locks or GUCs. The finding hinges on whether the unlock can plausibly fail while leaving the lock held.

Let me check whether the connection that ran setup() is actually the same one returned, and whether autocommit changes anything. The lock is acquired and unlocked on the same connection in autocommit mode. Let me verify the redundancy: even if `pg_advisory_unlock` fails, is there any other release mechanism? Let me check the test suite for what's asserted about this path.

content.text:
The tests use a fake pool that doesn't model real psycopg reset semantics. The finding's claim about real psycopg behavior stands in the fake-test world but tests don't disprove it. The critical question for refutation: does the connection that ran setup() actually get returned to the pool, or is it closed?

Let me reconsider the real mechanism carefully. The key refutation angle: **psycopg3 ConnectionPool, with `autocommit=True` connections, on `putconn` runs a reset.** Let me recall the actual psycopg 3.2 reset behavior precisely. In psycopg_pool's `ConnectionPool._reset_connection` (the default `reset` is `None`), the pool does:

```python
if conn.pgconn.transaction_status == ACTIVE/INTRANS/INERROR:
    conn.rollback()
```

For an autocommit connection that just ran a `SELECT`, the transaction status is `IDLE`, so even the rollback is skipped. **No `DISCARD ALL` is issued.** So a held session advisory lock would indeed survive return to the pool in real psycopg.

So the mechanism the finding describes is real *if* `pg_advisory_unlock` can fail while the lock is still held. Let me examine that part — is there a scenario where unlock raises but the lock is genuinely still held?

content.text:
Now let me think hard about the actual refutation. The finding requires `pg_advisory_unlock` to **raise an exception while the lock remains held**. 

Critical insight: `conn.execute(...)` on an **autocommit** connection. If `pg_advisory_unlock` raises, what would cause it? Options:
1. A network/connection error mid-statement — but then the connection is **broken**, and on return to the pool, psycopg's `check=ConnectionPool.check_connection` runs on **next checkout**, which would detect the dead connection and replace it. A broken connection → server-side session is gone → advisory lock auto-released by the server (session ended). So lock NOT retained.
2. A transient server error (e.g., serialization, deadlock) — but `pg_advisory_unlock` on a held lock in autocommit does not deadlock or serialize-fail; it's a simple session function.
3. `lock_timeout` causing the unlock to time out — `pg_advisory_unlock` does not wait, so lock_timeout doesn't apply.

The PoC monkeypatches `conn.execute` to throw only on the unlock statement — an artificial injection. But the question is whether a *real* transient failure can leave the lock held AND the connection reusable.

Let me verify the self-healing `check_connection` on checkout, which is the real refutation.

51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a

53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359

55	# Upper bound (milliseconds) on how long the cross-process advisory lock may be waited for
56	# during schema bootstrap. A stuck/crashed peer that still holds a live PG session on the lock
57	# must NOT be able to hang bootstrap forever (#129); lock_timeout aborts the wait. 0 disables
58	# the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
59	# an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value

68	    monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
69	    """
70	    raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")

content.text:
Now I have enough to reason precisely. Let me evaluate the two failure modes against the real psycopg pool semantics.

The finding's claim is: unlock fails → connection returned to pool still holding the lock → second process blocks for full `lock_timeout`.

Refutation analysis of whether a *realistic* failure leaves the lock held on a *reusable* connection:

**Case A — unlock fails due to connection breakage (network drop, server restart, OOM):** The PG session terminates. A terminated session **automatically releases all its session advisory locks** (PostgreSQL releases session-level advisory locks at session end). So the lock is gone. The broken connection, on next checkout, is caught by `check=ConnectionPool.check_connection` and replaced. No retained lock. Finding refuted in this case.

**Case B — unlock fails but connection stays alive (the PoC's artificial monkeypatch):** For the lock to remain held, the `pg_advisory_unlock` SQL must NOT have executed server-side, yet the client raised. In real psycopg autocommit, `conn.execute()` sends the query and reads the result in one round trip; an exception means either the statement failed server-side (lock still held, connection alive) or the wire broke (Case A). A *server-side* error on `SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(constant)` essentially cannot happen — it's a pure function on a valid bigint, no constraints, no locks-to-wait, no serialization. There is no realistic server-side error path. The PoC only achieves it by monkeypatching `conn.execute` to throw — i.e., injecting a fault that has no real analog.

So the finding's only path to a retained-lock-on-reusable-connection is an artificial fault injection with no realistic backing. But let me be rigorous: is there a *future-proofing* / defense-in-depth angle that makes this a legitimate latent bug regardless of probability? The `except Exception: pass` does swallow unconditionally, and the design *relies* on "auto-release-on-connection-return" — but the actual auto-release that protects the system is **PostgreSQL session-end release**, not pool-return release. The comment is imprecise but the protective behavior is real for the dangerous case (broken connection).

Let me double check there's no path where the SAME bootstrap connection (alive, lock held) is the one re-used AND psycopg doesn't reset. Even granting Case B were possible: would the lock actually block a second *process*? Yes — IF the lock is genuinely held. But Case B can't genuinely hold the lock after a real failure. The artificial monkeypatch makes `execute` a no-op that throws, so in the *fake* it never actually ran unlock — but in reality, if `execute` reaches the server it unlocks.

One more real-world subtlety: what if `pg_advisory_unlock` is sent but the **response** read fails (wire breaks after server processed it)? Then the server DID unlock, the connection is broken (Case A handling), lock released. Still refuted.

Let me confirm `check_connection` is the real psycopg method and runs on checkout (not just background), to nail Case A.

content.text:
I have enough to reach a confident conclusion. Let me synthesize the refutation.

The finding describes a real *code smell* (unconditional `except Exception: pass` on unlock, plus an imprecise comment about "auto-release-on-connection-return"). But the *security/correctness concern* — a pooled connection retaining the migration advisory lock and blocking a second process for the full `lock_timeout` — does not hold against real PostgreSQL + psycopg semantics. The PoC only achieves the retained-lock state by monkeypatching `conn.execute` to throw on the unlock statement, an injected fault with no realistic backing.

The decisive refutation:

1. **The only realistic way `pg_advisory_unlock` raises is a broken connection** (network drop / server restart / backend crash). When a PG session terminates, PostgreSQL **automatically releases all session-level advisory locks held by that session.** So the lock is gone the instant the failure that caused the raise occurs. The "second process blocks for full lock_timeout" scenario cannot materialize, because there is no surviving session holding the lock.

2. **`SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(<constant bigint>)` has no realistic server-side error path.** It is a pure function on a valid constant — no constraint violations, no waiting (advisory unlock never blocks, so `lock_timeout` is irrelevant to it), no serialization/deadlock failures in autocommit. There is no transient server-side error that returns an error response while leaving the lock held and the connection healthy. The PoC's "force unlock to raise while keeping the connection reusable" is physically unrealizable against real psycopg; it requires monkeypatching `execute` itself.

3. **The broken connection is self-healed before reuse.** Even in the broken-connection case, the pool is built with `check=ConnectionPool.check_connection`, which runs a liveness probe on every checkout and replaces a dead connection. So a broken bootstrap connection is never handed to a real checkpoint op.

4. **The lock_timeout GUC leak (C-1 cross-reference) is also moot for blocking other processes** — a GUC is session-local and does not affect any other process's `pg_advisory_lock` wait. (Separately, even within the same connection, the leaked GUC would only shorten/abort future lock waits, not extend them, and is not the mechanism this finding alleges.)

The finding's premise — "auto-release assumes the connection is CLOSED on return, but pooled connections are reset/reused, not closed" — is technically correct about psycopg's default reset (it does NOT issue `DISCARD ALL`, only a conditional rollback). But that observation only matters if the lock is ever genuinely held after the `finally` block runs, and it cannot be: either unlock succeeded (lock released), or unlock failed via a broken connection (session ended → server released the lock). The protective auto-release that actually saves the system is **session-end release**, which the comment names imprecisely but which is real and sufficient for the dangerous case.

input.reasoning:
REFUTED. The finding alleges a pooled bootstrap connection can retain the migration session advisory lock (because the best-effort unlock is swallowed and psycopg pools reset/reuse rather than close connections), thereby blocking a second process's pg_advisory_lock for the full lock_timeout. This cannot happen against real PostgreSQL + psycopg semantics; the PoC only achieves the retained-lock state by monkeypatching conn.execute to throw on the unlock statement — an injected fault with no realistic backing.

Decisive points (checked against src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:283-296 and the pool config at 266-278):

1. The ONLY realistic way `SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(<constant>)` raises is a broken connection (network drop, server restart, backend OOM/crash). When a PG session terminates, PostgreSQL AUTOMATICALLY releases all session-level advisory locks held by that session. So at the very moment the failure that caused the raise occurs, the lock is already gone — there is no surviving session to block a second process. The "blocks for full lock_timeout" scenario is unrealizable.

2. `pg_advisory_unlock` on a valid constant bigint in an autocommit connection has no realistic server-side error path: it is a pure function, never waits (so lock_timeout is irrelevant to it), and has no constraint/serialization/deadlock failure mode. There is no transient error that returns an error response while keeping the lock held AND the connection healthy. The PoC's required state (unlock raises, lock still held, connection still reusable) is physically impossible without monkeypatching execute itself.

3. Defense in depth: the pool is built with check=ConnectionPool.check_connection (line 276), a liveness probe on every checkout that replaces dead connections — so even a broken bootstrap connection is never handed to a real checkpoint op.

4. The cross-referenced lock_timeout GUC leak (C-1) is session-local and cannot extend any OTHER process's advisory-lock wait; it is not the alleged blocking mechanism.

The finding's underlying observation — that psycopg3's default pool reset does NOT issue DISCARD ALL (only a conditional rollback), so the comment 'auto-releases when the connection returns to the pool' is imprecise — is technically accurate. But it is moot: the protective auto-release that actually guards the system is PostgreSQL's session-END release, which is real and sufficient for the only failure case (broken connection) where unlock can plausibly raise. The worst genuine artifact here is an imprecise comment plus an unconditional except-pass, not the cross-process serialization defeat the finding claims.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial refutation of finding C-6 and submitted my conclusion via StructuredOutput.

**Verdict: REFUTED (survives=false)**

The finding alleges that a swallowed `pg_advisory_unlock` failure leaves a pooled connection holding the migration advisory lock, blocking a second process for the full `lock_timeout`. The decisive disproof:

- The only realistic way the unlock raises is a **broken connection**, and PostgreSQL **auto-releases all session-level advisory locks at session termination** — so the lock is gone the instant the failure occurs. No surviving session means nothing can block a second process.
- `SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(<constant>)` in autocommit has **no realistic server-side error path** (pure function, never waits, no constraint/serialization/deadlock failure). The PoC's required state — unlock raises while the lock stays held and the connection stays reusable — is only achievable by monkeypatching `conn.execute`, which has no real analog.
- `check=ConnectionPool.check_connection` (checkpoint.py:276) provides defense in depth, replacing any broken bootstrap connection on next checkout.

The finding's one accurate technical observation — that psycopg3's default pool reset does not `DISCARD ALL`, making the "auto-release on connection return" comment imprecise — is moot, because the protective release that actually matters is PostgreSQL's session-end release. The real residue is a cosmetic comment imprecision plus an unconditional `except Exception: pass`, not the cross-process serialization defeat alleged.

Relevant file: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:283-296`.
